name: Testing Workflow with pytest, Fixtures, and Coverage
description: Write and run Python tests with pytest, using fixtures for setup, parametrize for data-driven tests, and coverage for reporting.
trendingReason: pytest dominates Python testing with its simple assertion syntax and powerful fixture system, adopted by virtually every major Python project.
prerequisites: A Python project with virtual environment

steps.title: Install pytest and coverage
steps.description: Install the pytest framework, coverage plugin for code coverage reports, and xdist for parallel test execution.
steps.tip: pytest-xdist lets you run tests in parallel with -n auto, dramatically reducing test suite times on multi-core machines.

steps.title: Write and run a basic test
steps.description: Run all tests in the tests/ directory with verbose output showing each test name and result. pytest auto-discovers files matching test_*.py or *_test.py.

steps.title: Run tests matching a keyword
steps.description: Run only tests whose names match the given keyword expression. Useful for running a subset of tests during development.

steps.title: Run tests with coverage report
steps.description: Run the full test suite and generate a coverage report showing which lines in the src/ directory are not covered by tests.
steps.tip: Add --cov-fail-under=80 to fail the test run if coverage drops below 80 percent.

steps.title: Run tests in parallel
steps.description: Execute tests across all available CPU cores using pytest-xdist. This can cut test runtime by 50-80 percent on large suites.
steps.warning: Parallel tests must be independent. Tests that share global state, databases, or files may fail or produce flaky results when parallelized.

steps.title: Run only failed tests from the last run
steps.description: Re-run only the tests that failed in the previous execution. This is the fastest way to iterate on fixing broken tests.

faq.question: What is the difference between pytest fixtures and setUp/tearDown?
faq.answer: Fixtures are more flexible than unittest's setUp/tearDown. They support dependency injection, scoping (function, class, module, session), parametrization, and can be shared across files via conftest.py.

faq.question: How do I skip a test conditionally?
faq.answer: Use @pytest.mark.skip(reason='...') to always skip, or @pytest.mark.skipif(condition, reason='...') to skip based on a condition like platform or Python version.

faq.question: How do I test for expected exceptions?
faq.answer: Use the pytest.raises context manager: with pytest.raises(ValueError, match='invalid input'): my_function('bad_data'). This asserts that the exception is raised with a matching message.
